title: RN Respiratory Emergency Study Set

flashcards.front: A client with asthma is speaking in one-word phrases and using accessory muscles. What is the priority?
flashcards.back: Position upright, assess oxygenation and work of breathing, administer ordered rescue therapy, and escalate rapidly.
flashcards.rationale: Severe work of breathing indicates impending respiratory failure and requires immediate intervention.
flashcards.pearl: One-word speech is a high-risk respiratory distress cue.

flashcards.front: Which oxygenation finding requires urgent reassessment?
flashcards.back: A falling oxygen saturation with increasing drowsiness or reduced respiratory effort requires urgent reassessment.
flashcards.rationale: Mental status changes can reflect hypoxemia, hypercapnia, fatigue, or impending ventilatory failure.
flashcards.pearl: A quieter patient is not always an improving patient.

flashcards.front: What should the nurse verify before suctioning a tracheostomy?
flashcards.back: Assess oxygenation, explain the procedure as able, use appropriate sterile technique, and limit suction duration.
flashcards.rationale: Suctioning can worsen hypoxemia if performed too long or without preparation.
flashcards.pearl: Suction only as needed, not simply by the clock.

questions.stem: A client with COPD becomes acutely confused and increasingly somnolent. What is the priority?
questions.answer: Assess respiratory status, oxygenation, and ventilation immediately.
questions.rationale: New confusion can signal gas exchange failure or CO2 retention and requires rapid assessment.

pearls: Work of breathing often deteriorates before oxygen saturation drops.
Respiratory fatigue can look like sudden calmness.
